title: Today's Tech Brief: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice
summary: A look at the intersection of AI and spirituality, where half of US Christians are open to AI's spiritual guidance.

imageCaption: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice
imageSourceTitle: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice

body.text: I've been digging into some interesting developments at the intersection of technology and spirituality. It appears that a significant portion of US Christians are open to receiving spiritual advice from AI. According to a report from The Register, half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual guidance. This raises some fascinating questions about the role of technology in our personal lives. I mean, who would have thought that AI would become a spiritual advisor?

The report suggests that AI's increasing presence in spiritual contexts is not just about providing information, but also about offering guidance and support. This trend speaks to the evolving nature of both technology and spirituality. I'm also seeing how AI's capabilities are expanding beyond traditional applications. For instance, AI-powered chatbots are being used in mental health support, and now, spiritual guidance.

Another development that caught my attention is the use of AI in art and creativity. AI-generated art is becoming increasingly popular, and it's raising questions about the role of human creativity in the digital age. Additionally, there's been a surge in AI-powered music tools, which are changing the way musicians create and produce music. These advancements showcase the versatility of AI and its potential to transform various industries.

Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice is also worth a quick mention. America’s Christian population appears to have found God in precisely the place you’d expect a ma... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
